464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding & first experience** – No documented path from signup through first class to retention checkpoint; unclear who owns welcome calls, form setup, buddy matching, expectation-setting
- **Churn & win-back** – No trigger-based process for pause requests, cancellations, or dormant member re-engagement; currently reactive
- **Coaching standards & quality assurance** – No class quality audit, coaching consistency check, or feedback loop; coaching development roadmap missing
- **Member data, privacy & GDPR** – No documented handling of personal data, consent tracking, data deletion requests, or compliance audit trail
- **Complaints & incidents** – No escalation path, documentation standard, or resolution protocol for member safety, behaviour, or service issues
- **Supplies & inventory** – No ordering, stock-level, or supplier management process; likely ad-hoc
- **Finance & billing edge cases** – Unclear how payment failures, refunds, credits, or dispute resolution are handled
- **Milestone & outcome tracking** – No SOP for capturing member progress, 30-day goals, transformation stories, or data entry into system

**Needs updating**

- **Member retention/engagement SOPs** – Referral programme SOP likely missing mechanics (who tracks, incentive approval, payout timing); milestone achievements SOP unclear on who triggers celebrations and how
- **Coach onboarding/offboarding** – Weekly key coach tasks undefined; development roadmap missing; unclear on performance feedback cadence or capability gaps
- **Class launch & quarterly programming** – New quarter programme launch SOP may lack member communication timeline, class schedule change process, or coach prep handoff
- **Marketing SOPs** – 5 SOPs built but likely missing member feedback loops, campaign performance tracking, or referral source attribution back to retention

**Build next**

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Actions

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

- **AR/M collapsing: June £120.48 vs May £165.56 (27% drop).** This is your single biggest red flag. With 122→147 members (+20% headcount), revenue should have climbed £3–4k; instead it fell £2.5k. Members are paying less per month — either discounting new joiners, higher churn of premium-tier payers, or billing/collection lag. Audit: (1) what tier are the 25 new members on? (2) Are lapsed members still showing as active? (3) Any payment failures? Fix this first.
- **Churn spike: May 5.74% → June 0.00% is false.** Losing 7 members month-on-month (108→147 growth minus attrition) doesn't yield zero churn. Investigate data entry; likely you're seeing June incomplete or misreporting. True churn is probably 3–5%. Run retention campaigns on 90+ day cohorts (September–November cohort is now 8 months old; check if they're still active).
- **Upsell opportunity in bigger studio:** You now have 147 capacity headroom in a larger space. Introduce: (a) premium "peak hours" tier (+£8–12/month for prime slots), (b) personal training packages (£35–50 per session, 1–2 clients/week = +£500+/month if uptake reaches 10% of base), (c) retail (protein, water bottles, apparel — target 3–5% of revenue = £500–1k/month at zero cost). December's £170 AR/M shows this is achievable.
- **Pricing: June AR/M of £120 suggests base membership is £120.** Benchmark against competitors; if you're underselling, raise to £135–145 for *new* joiners (don't reprrice existing). Grandfather existing; new member onboarding (currently +25/month) at +£15 = +£375/month.

---

## EXPENSES — PEOPLE & NON-PEOPLE DIVERGENCE

- **Non-people expenses are the real problem.** May £9,233 vs June £9,390 (stable), BUT March–April spike (£15.6k, £30.5k during relocation) is masking ongoing high spend. June's £9.4k on £17.7k revenue = 53% non-people ratio vs healthy 30–35% target. Drill into: (1

• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately.** Her Build: Lower (17 sessions) should shift to JoJo; Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) to Eilis to build her confidence; Build: Upper (9) split between JoJo and Eilis. This alone frees Grace from ~38 hours of delivery.

• **Move Grace's PT load (equivalent) to JoJo and Eilis.** Grace ran 9 body comp scans (not real coaching); her real PT was minimal. JoJo takes on 4–5 additional PT slots, Eilis takes 3–4. Eilis needs PT experience to develop, and JoJo can absorb it given her capacity.

• **JoJo is over-loaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 44 PT sessions and most of the "Build" classes. Cap her at ~100 sessions by handing Eilis more Elevate and Full Body classes (Eilis ran only 6 last month). Eilis can safely step up here.

• **Plug the No Sweat Intro gap.** Grace ran 3; neither JoJo nor Eilis have taken these. Assign them alternately (JoJo 2, Eilis 1) to share induction load and free Grace completely.

• **Use body comp scans as a true admin task, not coaching time.** JoJo's 20 scans are inflating her session count. Assign scans to whoever has space that week; Eilis should take more of them to free JoJo for higher-value PT.

• **Agree a handover schedule with Eilis now** (e.g. "You lead all Elevate and Plate classes by end of July"). Without it, JoJo and E

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Launch a "Strength Basics" workshop series** on Monday and Thursday mornings (your quietest days) — 30-min drop-in sessions covering deadlift form, squat technique, or barbell setup. Position it as a prep for your Build classes to convert curious members into regular attendees.

2. **Open a paid PT block** Monday 6-7pm and Thursday 5-6pm when studios sit empty between scheduled classes — target members who want 1-on-1 coaching but haven't committed to packages yet.

**For the struggling classes:**

3. **Move "Strongevity" and "Barbelle"** to Wednesday or Friday (your peak attendance days) — even a weak class gains 40-50% more footfall on busy days. If timing conflicts exist, test moving one to an off-peak slot that feeds into a busier class back-to-back.

4. **Rebrand "Engine" and "Capacity"** with a single targeted invite to your "Build" class regulars (your strongest segment at 537+) — these conditioning classes complement strength work, so position them as the perfect pairing. Include a "try free" offer for one session.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your actual revenue streams TODAY**
— Don't design from scratch. List every service you currently sell (memberships, classes, PT, nutrition coaching, challenges, merchandise). Include what members actually buy vs. what you *think* they should. This grounds you in reality, not theory.

**2. Audit your coaches' knowledge gaps first**
— Before publishing anything, sit down with your coaches and ask: "What do members ask you about that you can't confidently answer?" (pricing tiers, what's included in memberships, package options). These gaps are your biggest clarity problems. Fix internal confusion before external messaging.

**3. Create two separate one-page documents (not a brochure)**
— **For coaches**: Pricing, package inclusions, what they can/can't promise, how to upsell. Make it a laminated desk reference, not a novel.
— **For members**: Simple tier comparison (e.g., "Basic £X/month = unlimited classes + gym access"; "Premium £X = all that + 1 PT session"). Use a table. No marketing copy yet.

**4. Validate pricing with your top 10 members first**
— Before finalizing, ask 3-4 long-term members: "Would you pay £X for this?" and "What's missing?" This prevents pricing mistakes that cost you months to fix.

**5. Post it where it matters (not everywhere)**
— Put the member version on your website, at signup, and in the gym entrance. Give coaches the internal version in their onboarding. You're done when a new member can understand your offering in 60 seconds.

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

1. **July is a breakthrough month, not a fluke.** You've cracked something fundamental: cost-per-lead dropped 90% (£21 to £2) while volume jumped 8x, *and* you converted 22 leads to sales for the first time. This isn't luck—your creative, targeting, or offer resonates now. April–June shows you were burning budget on wrong audience or weak creative.

2. **Your close rate (13%) is solid but the real story is lead quality.** Previous months generated expensive, cold leads that wouldn't convert. July's leads self-select differently—either messaging now attracts serious prospects, or you're reaching women further along in consideration. Either way, you've moved from quantity-chasing to quality.

3. **Profit per lead (£30.36) is healthy if sustainable.** At £376 spend for £60 profit, you're profitable *this month*, but that's thin margin. You need to scale spend without degrading unit economics—the risk is doubling budget and watching CPL creep back up to £5–7.

---

**Actions**

1. **Pause all Apr–Jun creative immediately and double-budget on whatever won in July.** You don't know *which* creative/copy/audience drove the shift yet—so audit your July campaigns granularly (by ad, audience, placement). Identify the top 2–3 performers by ROAS or conversions, then allocate 60% of new budget there. If you ran A/B tests in July, the winner gets first claim on extra spend.

2. **Test audience expansion *cautiously* with lookalike and interest stacking.** Create a 1% Lookalike of your 22 July converters (not just leads—actual sales). Run it at 15–20% of budget in August. Watch CPL weekly; if it stays under £3.50, scale. If it drifts above £5, kill it. Also test layering: women 25–45 + fitness interest + high-intent keywords (trial, "6-week challenge", gym membership Belfast). This tightens targeting without alienating new prospects.

3. **Increase August budget to £550–600 (

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## REVENUE — URGENT PRIORITY

• **Rev/member has collapsed to £118–£119 (June, May) from £130–£138 (Feb–Apr).** You're not pricing hard enough or upsells have dried up. Audit class pricing (especially peak times), PT packages, and merchandise. Test a £3–5 per-class premium for busy slots or a tiered membership (basic £25/month → premium £35 with unlimited peak access). Target £135+ Rev/member by August.

• **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May (after relocation) and was 11.8% in Dec.** This is killing growth. Post-move, you've lost 18 members in one month. Survey those who left (pricing, class times, facilities). Retention plan needed: welcome calls within 48h, drop-in guest passes for lapsed members, and a "friend for free" referral scheme. June's 3.3% is good — understand what changed and replicate it.

• **Active members plateaued at 134–135 (May–June) vs 112–122 pre-relocation.** New studio isn't pulling net new sign-ups fast enough. Launch a Q3 acquisition push: Instagram ads targeting women 25–45 in Belfast postcodes (start £300/month), partner with corporate wellness teams (3–5 bulk memberships), and offer 2-week free trial (not discount) to friends of members.

## EXPENSES — SECONDARY FOCUS

• **Cash burn in March (£-5,307), April (planned, £-6,819 — studio move), and June (£-2,521) shows tight working capital.** Excluding April, you're running £2–5k monthly operating losses. Check: are utilities higher post-relocation? Compare March–June payroll vs Nov–Feb. If staffing or facility costs jumped more than 15%, renegotiate lease, shift one PT session to freelance, or cut a low-attendance class (measure by member attendance per hour, not slot count). Target zero net cash burn by August.

• **Supplier spend isn't visible in your data, but if equipment/cleaning/utilities are >25% of revenue (currently ~£15.8k/month average), that's high.** Get three quotes on cleaning/maintenance contracts, review utility supplier, and negotiate equipment lease terms.

## PROFITABILITY & TAX

• **At £15.8k average monthly revenue (last 3 months) you're ~£190k annualised — well below VAT threshold (£90k/12
